title: Would you consider NGE to be Science Fiction
display_title: Would you consider NGE to be Science Fiction
wikitext:
[]: Would you consider NGE to be Science Fiction?             Quote Post by jesuspuffgirl69 » Wed Jan 08, 2014 11:09 pm In my Sci Fi class, we're debating the merits of work being defined as such or not. Often, the line is blurred or can be disputed over when some think a text is Science Fiction, whilst others deem the same text as just Fantasy. Considering the religious themes, amongst the other seemingly far-reaching attributes of the series, I feel that the Fantasy aspect can be debated here. Though I definitely consider Evangelion to be of many genres, I personally classify it as Science Fiction, not Fantasy, for reasons that I can discuss later. Right now, I'd like to know what you guys think. So, Eva: Sci Fi or Fantasy, and why?

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Jan 08, 2014 11:14 pm It's about magic, so no.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Jan 08, 2014 11:28 pm Evangelion , for all its esoteric references, makes very heavy use of a science fiction base. The back story is essentially "panspermia via intelligent, hyper-technological alien antecedents". And to play off Chuckman's claim, "Any sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic."

[]:             Quote Post by monitoradiation » Wed Jan 08, 2014 11:44 pm From a epistemological point of view, you presented us with a false dichotomy. You need direct logical negations. The true dichotomies are: 1. Sci-Fi vs Not Sci-Fi 2. Fantasy vs Not Fantasy I'd argue that it's both Sci-Fi and Fantasy, for the same reasons as Reichu have pointed out.

[]:             Quote Post by cyharding » Wed Jan 08, 2014 11:57 pm monitoradiation wrote: I'd argue that it's both Sci-Fi and Fantasy, for the same reasons as Reichu have pointed out.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Jan 09, 2014 12:04 am monitoradiation wrote: I'd argue that it's both Sci-Fi and Fantasy, for the same reasons as Reichu have pointed out.

[]:             Quote Post by monitoradiation » Thu Jan 09, 2014 12:11 am Reichu wrote: I actually brought up Clarke's third law because anything that seems inexplicably mystical or "magical" in NGE is just sufficiently advanced technology. This built-in demystification results in NGE following sci-fi genre conventions only, not those of fantasy .

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Jan 09, 2014 12:13 am Only if you insist on a ridiculously narrow definition of fantasy. The core conceit of Eva's world is that people have literal souls. Part of the narrative takes place in the afterlife. One of the characters transcends physical form and becomes a being unbound by space and time. These things are all innately mystical. Eva is partly a work of Lovecraftian horror. Any magic, if sufficiently debased, resembles science. In Eva, Man tries to constrain that which is beyond him with his petty devices and fails.

[]:             Quote Post by Rei IV » Thu Jan 09, 2014 1:27 am I don't know about the original series, but for NTE, especially Q, I would say definitely so. This is especially true for the depictions of the Evas in that movie, especially Mark.09 and Eva 13.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Jan 09, 2014 2:15 am It's unquestionably SF, including its invented discipline of metabiology and the associated engineering applications. The genre has been happy with psychic powers since the Gernsback days, near enough; NGE doesn't go that far towards hocus-pocus magic, and, by making the soul manipulation an engineering problem, is more SF than most in that respect. The kaiju/super-robot skin is an artefact of being Japanese; the rest of the story (in either TV or EoE end) fits very well into the somewhat millennial strands of SF that were noticeable in the closing decades of the last century.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Thu Jan 09, 2014 1:18 pm Reichu wrote: I guess mileage may vary on whether NGE's sci-fi is so soft it falls into science fantasy.

[]:             Quote Post by Dataprime » Thu Jan 09, 2014 1:31 pm My rule of thumb is if a show has Robots in it. It's Science Fiction.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Jan 09, 2014 1:43 pm Chuckman wrote: It's about magic, so no.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Jan 09, 2014 6:15 pm Dataprime wrote: My rule of thumb is if a show has Robots in it. It's Science Fiction.

[]:             Quote Post by TMBounty_Hunter » Thu Jan 09, 2014 6:26 pm Dataprime wrote: My rule of thumb is if a show has Robots in it. It's Science Fiction.

[]:             Quote Post by Guy Nacks » Fri Jan 10, 2014 12:07 am Yes, because NGE incorporates a lot of science and technobabble into their storylines and at the very least tries to explain or give hints as to how the stuff works without it just existing for the sake of existing.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Fri Jan 10, 2014 1:31 am Thread surgery complete. The winding tangent I've dubbed "Kaji as James Bond, Nerv as Evil Organization" ended up in the graveyard unless someone thinks it really deserves life somewhere else.

[]:             Quote Post by Tribblepoo » Fri Jan 10, 2014 2:57 am NGE is, at its core, about the impact of a futuristic setting and/or element and the human condition on each other; the classical definition of science fiction. Now one has to remember that there are essentially two kinds of science fiction; "hard" and "soft" sci-fi. "Hard" science fiction sticks to the laws of physics (as we know them) to create the futuristic settings. As a result, the futuristic setting is less fantastic in a way because it incorporates elements of what we already know and are familiar with. On the other hand, "soft" science fiction takes a hint from fantasy writing; namely, if an element makes for a good story, stick it in, physics be damned. However, soft sci-fi still differs from fantasy in that science fiction tends to be more about the characters and fantasy focuses more on the adventure. This is where the debate about Star Wars comes in, because it really does toe that line between soft sci-fi and fantasy. NGE definitely falls within the "soft sci-fi" category, but it has all of the elements of sci-fi strongly and not so much the elements of fantasy.

[]:             Quote Post by Dataprime » Fri Jan 10, 2014 10:38 am Reichu wrote: Hate to break this to you, but robots can and do show up in fantasy . (Also, you'd better not be implying that the Evas are robots.)

[]:             Quote Post by CJD » Fri Jan 10, 2014 12:25 pm The Eva Monkey wrote: Evangelion is a lot of things, I think mainly because Anno was constantly reassessing what he wanted to do. But if you had to narrowly define the genre, I would science fiction/psychological drama .
